attributes.stem: An otherwise well 55-year-old, post-menopausal woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a painless lump in her left breast. On examination, there is a 3 cm diameter firm, irregular mass in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast, tethered to the underlying muscle and 4 cm from the nipple. Examination of the left axilla reveals palpable nodes in the apical and pectoral groups. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. Diagnostic mammography shows an irregular, spiculated mass with microcalcifications present. Breast ultrasound confirms three separate foci suspicious for carcinoma in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. Core biopsy of the main mass confirms invasive ductal carcinoma which is oestrogen receptor (ER)/progesterone receptor (PR) positive and HER2 receptor negative. Fine needle biopsy of one of the enlarged axillary lymph nodes confirms malignant cells.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate neoadjuvant therapy?
attributes.explanation:
The presence of malignant cells in the axillary lymph nodes indicates nodal involvement, which is crucial for staging and treatment planning. Surgery, often following neoadjuvant therapy, will address both the primary tumour and the affected lymph nodes to achieve local control of the disease.

The patient has ER/PR-positive and HER2-negative breast cancer, making anastrozole, an aromatase inhibitor, the most appropriate neoadjuvant therapy for a post-menopausal woman. Aromatase inhibitors are preferred over selective oestrogen receptor modulators like tamoxifen due to a lower recurrence risk in this group. Neoadjuvant therapy aims to shrink the tumour and affected lymph nodes, potentially allowing for breast-conserving surgery instead of mastectomy.

Tamoxifen is a selective oestrogen receptor modulator that could be used for ER-positive breast cancer, but it is less preferred than aromatase inhibitors in post-menopausal women.

Chemotherapy is typically reserved for triple-negative or HER2-positive breast cancers, not for ER/PR-positive, HER2-negative cases like this one. However, it may be considered if the tumour is large or if there is significant nodal involvement.

Trastuzumab is used for HER2-positive breast cancer, which is not applicable here as the patient is HER2-negative.

Raloxifen is not typically used as a neoadjuvant therapy for breast cancer; it is more commonly used for osteoporosis prevention in post-menopausal women.
learningPoints: The choice of neoadjuvant therapy for breast cancer will be based on the hormone receptor status.
Aromatase inhibitors are preferred for post-menopausal women with ER-positive breast cancer due to a reduced recurrence risk.
Neoadjuvant therapy can shrink the tumour and affected lymph nodes, potentially allowing for breast-conserving surgery.
Assessment of axillary lymph nodes is crucial in staging and planning treatment for breast cancer.
references: Heilat GB, Brennan ME, French J. Update on the management of early-stage breast cancer. Aust J Gen Pract. 2019 Sep;48(9):604-608. doi: 10.31128/AJGP-03-19-4891. PMID: 31476829.

Dixon JM, Renshaw L, Bellamy C, Stuart M, Hoctin-Boes G, Miller WR. The effects of neoadjuvant anastrozole (Arimidex) on tumor volume in postmenopausal women with breast cancer: a randomized, double-blind, single-center study. Clin Cancer Res. 2000 Jun;6(6):2229-35. PMID: 10873072.
Kelly CM, Buzdar AU. Anastrozole. Expert Opin Drug Saf. 2010 Nov;9(6):995-1003. doi: 10.1517/14740338.2010.515977. PMID: 20923259.
